solutions.solution:
"In the park on a bench, a man was reading the newspaper when opportunity knocks." Do you know everytime I breathe, someone dies?" "The other man answered, "..." I. Have you ever tried mouthwash? II. Practise deep and longer breathing. At least every second person dies.

Solution: We see that both statements fit as punchlines. First, both are the other person's breaths both in such a way that it makes the breath appear as if that man's breath was the cause of death. Second, both are unexpected and have some humour in it. When the man reads that the time he takes to die is the same to the other person. We expect the other person to give a shocking answer which means that the man's breath is of death; it is an unexpected twist. Now, both statements fit as punchlines. It means that the choice is either (c) or (d). Let us now take each statement and ask ourselves this question: "What makes it funny?" When we ask this question about I, we find that the other man's implying that the first person's breath was the cause of death, makes it funny. We see that the same logic was behind II as well. It means that both of them fit with the same idea. The correct answer is (c).

The cleaning lady of a bank gave notice saying: "You do not trust me." The branch manager replied; "How can you say that? I even have keys of the safe lying around." Said the cleaning lady.........

Solution: Here, statement I and II both fit as punchlines. Both are funny because the lady first raises objection to her being mistrusted and then adds that no cash is present in the safe or that the keys of all safe do not fit. It is unexpected because we do not expect a woman who is showing indignation over her master's mistrust to reveal the real reasons behind the master's action.

One afternoon, a customer came into the shop and hurriedly said, "A punchline is about to take place. But quietly. I have to catch the next bus." The shopkeeper replied; "But there are no mice in the bus." I. The other didn't have that big a mousetrap." Solution: Here statement I does not fit as a punchline. It is not funny. It is literally an unexpected answer but it does not make us laugh. There is nothing in the customer's statement which leaves room for creating such a joke. He does leave room for cuning a joke like II here says that he has to "catch the next bus." The shopkeeper may misinterpret it and say that he does not have mousetraps big enough to catch buses or that he fits as a punchline. But I does not. Hence, correct answer is (b).

Two little boys visit a doctor: "We swallowed a marble," said one boy. "Oh, yes," replied the doctor, "and is this your brother with you?" said the other boy,... L: "from the marble." II: "I saw him swallow it." Solution: Here, I fits as a punchline. It is funny and unexpected because what it effectively states is that the other child has accompanied the first child only to reclaim his marble! But II does not fit. It is not funny. The boy saw him swallow a marble and the two have come together; it is a plain fact and there can be no humour in it. Correct answer (a).

My wife and I always take decisions with consensus, ... L: ...she proposes and I agree. II: ...she beats me if we don't agree. Solution: Both fit as punchlines. The opening part of the statement merely mentions a husband describing an aspect of marital relationship. And we expect the sentence to be completed as something like "...", both of us understand each other so well". But as it turns out in both the cases, the meaning of consensus is forced agreement in which the wife's will dominates. This is funny. And both of them serve the same idea. Correct answer is (c). Note Ex. 16, 17, 18, 19, 20 have actually appeared in a PO exam. Final Note: This article has now given you a complete method of solving the punchline problem. A step-by-step approach has been outlined and this should make you a master of punchline problems. However, as the first step of testing whether a statement fits as a punchline is the most crucial step. Let us take another look at this step.
